document.file: ./pdf-files/Evaluation of the water defluoridating potential of brushite-calcite and two indigenous bioadsorbent materials.pdf
document.title: Water defluoridation by brushite-calcite and two indigenous bioadsorbents Lakshminarayan, Giriraju 27 27 EVALUATION OF THE WATER DEFLUORIDATING POTENTIAL OF BRUSHITE-CALCITE AND TWO INDIGENOUS BIOADSORBENT MATERIALS
document.img_preview_path: ./pdf-preview-images/Evaluation of the water defluoridating potential of brushite-calcite and two indigenous bioadsorbent materials.jpeg
document.abstract: The aim of this study was to compare the water defluoridating capability of the brushite-calcite combination reported by Larsen and Pearce with that of two locally available bioadsorbents. A suspension of 0.5 g each of brushite and calcite in 1.0 L of distilled water containing 5.0 ppm fluoride (from NaF) was boiled for 30 sec and then allowed to cool and stand for sedimentation to occur. By the fluoride ion selective electrode method, the F concentration in the water was 1.2 ppm after 90 min and 1.0 ppm after 18 hr, essentially in agreement with the results of Larsen and Pearce. For defluoridation by the bioadsorbents, 10 g each of dried and powdered neem or pipal leaves pretreated with NaOH was suspended in a cloth bag in 1.0 L of distilled water containing 5 ppm F. After 90 min the F concentration in the water was 4.0 ppm and 3.2 ppm after 18 hr, indicating a much lower defluoridating potential than with brushite-calcite.

method.heading: MATERIALS AND METHODS
method.text: A solution of 5 ppm F prepared by dissolving 22.0 mg of NaF in 2.0 L of distilled water was divided in half and labeled Solution A and Solution B. For the first part of the study, 500 mg of brushite and 500 mg of calcite were suspended in Solution A, which was then boiled for 30 seconds to facilitate sedimentation of calcium salts. 3 After 90 min and at 18 hr, samples were drawn from this solution and assessed for the F ion concentration by a Jenway 3330 Research meter with an ion selective electrode. For the second part of the study, a 10-g portion of chemically treated leaf biomass (dried, ground neem and pipal leaves treated by boiling for 30 min in 400 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution and rinsed with 1000 mL of distilled water was suspended in a cloth bag in Solution B, followed by F analysis after 90 min and at 18 hr as for Solution A.

document.full_text:
Since excessive intake of fluoride (F) has the potential to cause not only dental and skeletal but also other forms of fluorosis, defluoridation of drinking water is matter of great importance in many parts of the world. This concern in turn has led to efforts to find effective and economical ways to remove F from drinking water. 1 During the past several decades, extensive research has focused on a number of alternative adsorbents, some exhibiting better F sorption capability than others while at the same time not diminishing the quality of the treated water. 2 The present study re-examines the defluoridating potential of the brushite-calcite combination reported in 2002 by Larsen and Pearce 3 and also the use of bioadsorbents 4 with two locally available materials, dried and ground neem and pipal leaves, for removing F from water.
A solution of 5 ppm F prepared by dissolving 22.0 mg of NaF in 2.0 L of distilled water was divided in half and labeled Solution A and Solution B. For the first part of the study, 500 mg of brushite and 500 mg of calcite were suspended in Solution A, which was then boiled for 30 seconds to facilitate sedimentation of calcium salts. 3 After 90 min and at 18 hr, samples were drawn from this solution and assessed for the F ion concentration by a Jenway 3330 Research meter with an ion selective electrode. For the second part of the study, a 10-g portion of chemically treated leaf biomass (dried, ground neem and pipal leaves treated by boiling for 30 min in 400 mL of 0.1 M NaOH solution and rinsed with 1000 mL of distilled water was suspended in a cloth bag in Solution B, followed by F analysis after 90 min and at 18 hr as for Solution A.
In Solution A treated with brushite and calcite, the F concentration was reduced from 5 ppm to 1.2 ppm in 90 min and decreased to 1.0 ppm after 18 hr, indicating a substantial removal of F over a period of 90 min. In Solution B, using locally available bioadsorbant materials (dried, ground neem and pipal leaves), the F concentration was reduced from 5 ppm to 4 ppm in 90 min and decreased to 3.22 ppm after 18 hr, indicating a limited potential to defluoridate water.
Compared with the locally available bioadsorbent material, brushite-calcite as reported by Larsen and Pearce 3 proved to be superior for defluoridating water. These authors found that distilled water initially containing 5, 10, and 20 ppm F was ultimately reduced to 0.06, 0.4, and 5.9 ppm F, respectively. 3 In a follow-up study of the defluoridation of water at high pH by Larsen, Pearce, and Jensen, 5 a combination of brushite, calcium hydroxide, and bone char gave less reduction of F ion concentration compared to the results of the present study. 5 By our estimate, the cost of defluoridating water by the brushite-calcite method is 0.40 Rupee/L of water, compared to the retail market price of a 12 to 16 Rupees/L for low-F bottled water, thus showing that the brushite-calcite method is highly cost-effective.
Previous studies using locally available bioadsorbant materials for defluoridating water have reported better results than in the present study. 4,[7][8][9][10][11][12] The reasons may be due to differences in the nature and potential of the adsorbent agents, pH, temperature, and water composition in those studies. Here, using the locally available either neem or pipal leaf bioadsorbents, we found that the F concentration of 3 L of fluoridated water can be partially reduced by about 30% at a cost of about 5 to 8 Rupees/L, including the cost of the 400 mL of 0.1 M NaOH and 1000 mL of distilled water needed to prepare the bioadsorbent material. With large-scale commercial manufacture of these dried bioadsorbents and their being made available and sold for domestic use, especially in villages, they can become comparatively cost effective.
Overall, both materials used in this study have the advantage of requiring minimal technical skill. Moreover, they are readily available, cause little change in the pH of the treated water, and their residue after use is easy to dispose.
